text.en: Who is speaking these words, and in what city is he king?
The Teacher, the son of David, king in Jerusalem (v1).
In verse 5, name in order the three things the sun does.
It rises, then it sets, then it hurries back to where it rises (v5): “The sun rises and the sun sets; it hurries back to where it rises.”
Draw one picture of the sun coming up and one of the sun going down, the way verse 5 says. Then point to which one happens first.
Either order can start the picture, but per v5 the stated order is the sun rises, then sets, then hurries back to rise again. Affirm the child’s drawing and re-read v5 together.
The Teacher opens with “Futility of futilities.” What is he saying about everything?
That everything is futile (v2). The doubled form “futility of futilities” is the passage’s way of saying the very utmost, most complete futility — the repetition intensifies the meaning.
The phrase “under the sun” appears in verses 3 and 9. What does the Teacher mean by it?

This present life in this present world — things as they are here below, considered on their own. It is a phrase peculiar to Ecclesiastes; keep the sense to ‘this life under heaven’ rather than importing outside definitions (vv3, 9).
Verse 3 asks, “What does a man gain from all his labor, at which he toils under the sun?” What does “gain” mean here, and how does the rest of the passage answer the question?

“Gain” is real, lasting profit — a benefit that remains. The passage answers by observation: generations pass, sun and wind and rivers only return where they began, “there is nothing new under the sun” (v9), and even remembrance fails (v11). So labor under the sun, taken by itself, yields no abiding gain. Anchor the answer in what the verses observe; do not speculate beyond them.
“Futility of futilities,” says the Teacher, “futility of futilities! Everything is futile!” (Ecclesiastes 1:2)
What has been will be again, and what has been done will be done again; there is nothing new under the sun. (Ecclesiastes 1:9)
Finish the Teacher’s line from verse 9: there is nothing new under the ____.
“sun” — “there is nothing new under the sun” (v9).
As the passage is read aloud, listen for the phrase “under the sun.” Raise your hand each time you hear it.
“under the sun” occurs in v3 and v9 — two times in this pericope.
As verse 2 is read aloud, listen for the word the Teacher repeats to describe everything, and count how many times you hear it.
futility / futile — in v2 the root sounds five times: “Futility of futilities,” says the Teacher, “futility of futilities! Everything is futile!” (futility, futilities, futility, futilities, futile).
Verse 9 says “What has been will be again.” Later in Ecclesiastes, one verse ties this same returning cycle to God’s own ordering. Which verse is it?

Ecclesiastes 3:15 — “What exists has already been, and what will be has already been” — sets the cycle of 1:9 within God’s ordering. Name that reference; the point of contact is the recurring “what has been”.
Verse 4 says generations come and go “but the earth remains forever.” Which psalm also speaks of God fixing the earth on its foundations so that it endures?
Psalm 104:5 — God set the earth on its foundations so that it should not be moved. It matches Ecclesiastes 1:4’s picture of the earth abiding while generations pass. Name Psalm 104:5.
The Teacher says everything is “futile.” Write down one question you would want to ask him about that.

Keep this fully open — the aim is the child’s own wondering, not a target answer. Welcome any honest question and, if helpful, invite them to look back at the verses that prompted it. Do not steer toward a ‘right’ question or supply the answer yourself.
The Teacher keeps saying “under the sun.” What question does that repeated phrase raise for you?

Leave this open. Many learners will wonder what lies beyond ‘under the sun,’ but let the question be theirs — don’t pre-load the answer. If they ask, point them back to how the phrase is used in vv3 and 9 and let them keep wondering.

Verses 5–7 describe the sun, the wind, and the rivers, and each keeps returning to where it began. Reading these alongside verse 3 (“What does a man gain…”) and verse 8, what do these tireless-yet-returning motions picture about human labor?

First fix what the text states: sun rises and sets and hurries back (v5), wind swirls round and returns on its course (v6), rivers run to the sea yet the sea is never full and the streams flow again (v7). Then read them as figures for human toil — endless, circular striving that never arrives, which is why v8 turns straight back to man: “All things are wearisome, more than one can describe; the eye is not satisfied with seeing, nor the ear content with hearing.” Keep the interpretation tied to these verses; don’t import a moral the text doesn’t state.
Verse 8 says “the eye is not satisfied with seeing, nor the ear content with hearing.” Why might the Teacher place this right after the rivers that flow yet never fill the sea (v7)?

Anchor first in what v7 and v8 say: the sea is never full though all rivers pour in; the eye is never satisfied though it keeps seeing. The nearness suggests the same pattern moving from nature to the person — restless taking-in that never reaches ‘full.’ Let learners state the link from the text; affirm readings grounded in vv7–8 rather than a single required wording.
In your own words, retell verses 1–11: who is speaking, the one question he asks about labor, the things in nature he points to, and what he concludes about anything being new.

Keep this open — check the retelling against the text rather than grading it to a script. Together, see whether it touched: the speaker, the Teacher/son of David/king in Jerusalem (v1); his thesis that all is futile (v2); the question “What does a man gain from all his labor, at which he toils under the sun?” (v3); generations passing while the earth remains (v4); the sun, wind, and rivers that keep returning (vv5–7); weariness and the unsatisfied eye and ear (v8); and “nothing new under the sun,” with remembrance failing (vv9–11). If a part was missing, have them re-read it and add it — don’t supply a model retelling.

Over the next day, keep a short written list of tasks you do that you also did yesterday. Bring the list, and beside it write one sentence on how verse 9 (“there is nothing new under the sun”) reads against your own week.

This asks for an observable action — a written list and one written sentence — not a self-assessment. Receive whatever is brought; do not push toward gloom or grade the reflection. The point is simply to notice the passage’s theme of recurring labor in one’s own ordinary week, staying with what v9 actually says.
What does the speaker call himself, and over which nation and city was he king?
He calls himself the Teacher, and he was king over Israel in Jerusalem (v12).
As you listen, catch how the speaker names himself and the land and city he ruled.
The Teacher, who was king over Israel in Jerusalem (v12).
The Teacher twice calls what he found "a pursuit of the wind." What does that image say about what he chased?
Wind cannot be caught or held; the image says his findings proved empty and could not be grasped or kept, however hard he searched (v14, v17).
The Teacher searches all that is done "under heaven" and "under the sun." What sphere of things do these repeated phrases mark out?
The whole realm of human works and life in this present world, which he set his mind to seek and explore (v13-14).
Listen for the same short phrase the Teacher uses again and again to describe what he found.
"A pursuit of the wind," said in both v14 and v17.
For with much wisdom comes much sorrow, and as knowledge grows, grief increases. (Ecclesiastes 1:18)
Fill in the Teacher's words from verse 15 — What is crooked cannot be ______, and what is lacking cannot be ______.
"straightened" and "counted" (v15).
The Teacher found that more wisdom brought more sorrow. According to Job 28:28, named in the study notes, what is the true wisdom his search under the sun kept missing?
Job 28:28 declares that the fear of the Lord, that is wisdom, and to turn away from evil is understanding.

In verse 14 the Teacher looks at all things done under the sun and finds them futile. When he surveys his own accomplishments again in Ecclesiastes 2:11 (see the study notes), what verdict does he reach there?
The same verdict: Ecclesiastes 2:11 says he found everything to be futile, a pursuit of the wind, with nothing to be gained under the sun.
The Teacher says that as his knowledge grew, so did his grief. What questions does that stir up in you about the things you most want to learn?

Let learners voice their own wondering; do not supply a verdict. Notice questions raised, without judging the faith behind them. Honest wonderings might touch why learning can bring pain, or where lasting satisfaction is found.
As you listen, write down one honest question you would want to ask the Teacher about his search and what he found.
This prompt gathers the learner's own question, not a right answer; receive whatever question is written and note it as evidence of engagement, not as a measure of the person.
The Teacher had grown "in wisdom beyond all those before" him, yet still calls his search futile. From the passage itself, why could even his great wisdom not satisfy him or mend the world?

Anchor answers in the text: his wisdom could not straighten what is crooked nor supply what is lacking (v15), and the more he knew, the more his sorrow and grief increased (v18). Human wisdom, excellent in its place, cannot by itself satisfy the soul or reform a fallen world; that is why he names it a burden and a pursuit of the wind.
Retell this passage in your own words, as if you were the Teacher: what you set out to do, what you found, and what you concluded about it.

A good retelling includes the setup (a king in Jerusalem setting his mind to seek out everything by wisdom), the finding (all of it futile, a pursuit of the wind, unable to straighten the crooked), and the conclusion (more wisdom brought more sorrow). Listen for the shape of the argument, not exact words.
Name one specific matter this week that you will bring to God in prayer instead of trying to solve it by your own cleverness alone, and write down when you will do it.

Look for a concrete, doable action (a named matter and a set time), not a statement about the person's faith. Record only what they plan to do; the passage's point is that human wisdom alone cannot straighten the crooked, so turning a matter to God is a fitting, observable response.

Draw a picture of someone trying to catch the wind with their hands. Then talk about it: the Teacher searched and searched, and said everything he found was like chasing the wind, because you cannot hold it. (For older children: make two columns on paper, one for what the Teacher studied and one for the words he used for what he found, "a pursuit of the wind.")

Wind cannot be caught, so the picture shows why the Teacher's search felt empty. For the older-child columns: what he studied includes wisdom, knowledge, and madness and folly (v13, v16, v17); what he found is "a pursuit of the wind" (v14, v17).
In which city did the writer say he became greater than everyone who had lived before him?
Jerusalem — he surpassed all in Jerusalem who had preceded him (v9; also v7).
The writer built and planted many things for himself. Name one thing he built or planted.
Any one of: houses, vineyards (v4); gardens, parks, fruit trees (v5); reservoirs / groves of trees (v6).
Draw one thing the writer made in the passage: a house, a vineyard, a garden, or a pool of water for the trees.
All four appear in the text: houses and vineyards (v4), gardens/parks (v5), and reservoirs to water the groves (v6). Any faithful drawing is right; help pre-readers name what they drew.
In this passage, which comes first: the writer building his houses and gardens, or his conclusion that everything was futile?
The building comes first — he lists all he built and gathered (v4-10), and only at the end, on review, delivers the verdict: “Yet when I considered all the works...” (v11).
The writer keeps calling his results “futile.” From how he uses the word here, what does he mean his pleasures and works failed to give him?
Not that the things were evil, but that they yielded no solid, lasting satisfaction — “nothing to be gained under the sun” (v11). Sought as the highest good, created goods leave the soul empty.
The writer weighs what is worthwhile “under heaven” and finds nothing gained “under the sun.” What horizon of life do those phrases point to?
This-worldly life and labor — what men do “under heaven during the few days of their lives” (v3), the earthly frame within which he ran his test (v11).
The writer says all his works were “a pursuit of the wind.” What is it like to chase after the wind?
You can run after wind but never catch it or hold it — you end with nothing in your hands. That is his picture of works that leave “nothing to be gained” (v11).

“Yet when I considered all the works that my hands had accomplished and what I had toiled to achieve, I found everything to be futile, a pursuit of the wind; there was nothing to be gained under the sun.” (Ecclesiastes 2:11)
I said to myself, “Come now, I will test you with pleasure; enjoy what is good!” But it proved to be futile. (Ecclesiastes 2:1)
Finish the writer's words from verse 1: Come now, I will test you with ___.
“pleasure” — “Come now, I will test you with pleasure; enjoy what is good!” (v1).
Verse 11 ends “futile, a pursuit of the wind.” This is the book's recurring refrain. Where earlier in Ecclesiastes 1 is that same verdict first stated?
Ecclesiastes 1:14 — there he first says he “found them all to be futile, a pursuit of the wind” (named in the brief as the book's refrain). Verse 2:11 repeats it after the pleasure experiment.

In verse 1 the writer talks to himself: “Come now, I will test you with pleasure; enjoy what is good!” A rich man in one of Jesus' parables speaks the same way to his own soul. Which parable, and where?

The Parable of the Rich Fool, Luke 12:19 (named in the brief), where the man tells his soul to take it easy and eat, drink, and be merry. Same self-address to pleasure, and God calls him a fool that night.
The writer gave his eyes and heart every desire (v10) yet gained nothing lasting. Which New Testament letter says the lust of the eyes and flesh and the pride of life are passing away?
1 John 2:16-17 (named in the brief) — the desire of the flesh, the desire of the eyes, and the pride of life pass away, but whoever does God's will abides forever.
The writer had anything his eyes wanted, yet still was not truly happy. What do you wonder about when you read that?

Keep this open — you are drawing out the child's own wondering, not steering to a set answer. Welcome any honest question (Why wasn't he happy? What would make him happy? Is wanting things bad?). Write the questions down; you don't need to resolve them here.
The writer ran a deliberate experiment on pleasure and reported the result. What question would you most want to put to him after reading his conclusion in verse 11?

This invites the learner's own question — do not supply one. Note where each person's question lands (the method of the test, the honesty of the verdict, what he thinks IS worthwhile) and let those questions steer the family's later reading rather than closing them off.
According to verse 11, when the writer reviewed everything his hands had done, what did he say was “gained under the sun”? Why do you think all that pleasure and work still left him empty?

Anchor first in the text: he says there was “nothing to be gained under the sun” (v11). Then let them reason from what the passage shows — every good was sought “for myself” (v4-8) and weighed only “under the sun,” so created things, made the highest good, cannot fill the soul. Don't push past what the text plainly says.

Even while testing wine and pleasure, the writer notes his “mind still guiding me with wisdom” (v3) and that “my wisdom remained with me” (v9). Why does his staying clear-headed make his final verdict more trustworthy?

The text twice insists he kept his wisdom (v3, v9): this was a controlled trial, not a drunkard's slide. He stayed master of his pleasures rather than their slave, so he is a competent, sober judge of them — which is why his “nothing to be gained” (v11) carries weight. Keep the reasoning tied to those verses.
In your own words, retell the whole passage (verses 1-11): what the writer set out to test, the things he built and gathered, and what he concluded when he looked back over all of it.

Keep this open — check the retelling against the text, don't grade it against a script. Look together for: the plan to test pleasure (v1-3); the catalog of works, wealth, and delights (v4-10); and the closing verdict that it was all futile, with nothing gained (v11). If a part is missing, re-read it and let them add it.
On an index card, write or draw one thing you spend a lot of time or effort chasing after this week. Underneath, copy what verse 11 says was “gained under the sun.”

This asks for a concrete, observable action, not a judgment of anyone's heart. The line they copy is “nothing to be gained under the sun” (v11). Let the card prompt reflection; do not turn it into an assessment of whether the child's pursuit is good or bad.
Listen for all the different things the writer builds, makes, or gathers for himself.

Houses, vineyards (v4); gardens, parks, fruit trees (v5); reservoirs and groves (v6); menservants, maidservants, herds and flocks (v7); silver, gold, treasure, singers (v8). Any several they catch is a good listen.
Listen for the word “futile” and the phrase “pursuit of the wind” — where do they fall in the passage?
“Futile” frames the whole test: it opens the experiment (v1) and closes the verdict (v11), where it pairs with “a pursuit of the wind.” The word bookends everything in between.
Listen for the moment the writer turns from listing his works to judging them, and note one question that turn raises for you.

The turn is “Yet when I considered all the works...” (v11), where enumeration becomes retrospective judgment. This listen-for asks for the learner's own question, so welcome whatever it surfaces rather than supplying one.
A central contrast in this passage is between the wise man and the fool. According to verses 13-14, what does the Preacher say sets them apart?
He distinguishes them by sight and darkness: 'The wise man has eyes in his head, but the fool walks in darkness' (v.14), because 'wisdom exceeds folly, just as light exceeds darkness' (v.13).

verse.en: The wise man has eyes in his head, but the fool walks in darkness. Yet I also came to realize that one fate overcomes them both.

text.en: In the closing verses (24-26), who does the passage name as the one who gives the good of enjoyment, and to whom does He give it?
God is the giver: the enjoyment of food, drink, and work is 'from the hand of God' (v.24), and 'To the man who is pleasing in His sight, He gives wisdom and knowledge and joy' (v.26).

verse.en: To the man who is pleasing in His sight, He gives wisdom and knowledge and joy, but to the sinner He assigns the task of gathering and accumulating that which he will hand over to one who pleases God.

text.en: As the passage is read, listen for the key contrast the Preacher draws between the one who sees and the one who walks in the dark.
The contrast is between the wise man and the fool (vv.13-14): 'The wise man has eyes in his head, but the fool walks in darkness' (v.14), yet 'one fate overcomes them both' (v.14).

The Preacher's thought moves in steps. Put these in the order the passage gives them: he hated life; he turned to consider wisdom and folly; he saw that wisdom exceeds folly; he concluded that nothing is better than to enjoy one's work as from God's hand; he realized one fate overcomes the wise man and the fool alike.

Order: (1) 'Then I turned to consider wisdom and madness and folly' (v.12); (2) 'I saw that wisdom exceeds folly' (v.13); (3) 'one fate overcomes them both' (v.14); (4) 'So I hated life' (v.17); (5) 'Nothing is better for a man than to eat and drink and enjoy his work... this is from the hand of God' (v.24).
The phrase 'under the sun' recurs through the passage (vv.17-22). What horizon or realm is the Preacher pointing to with it?
'Under the sun' marks the horizon of this present life, where the whole trial of toil and wisdom is confined. Within that horizon he weighs everything and finds no lasting gain (vv.11, 17-22).
Verse 14 says 'one fate overcomes them both.' Using verse 16, what is the 'one fate' that befalls wise man and fool alike?
The one fate is death. Verse 16 makes it plain: 'the wise man will die just like the fool!' Both die, and both are forgotten in the days to come.

verse.en: For there is no lasting remembrance of the wise, just as with the fool, seeing that both will be forgotten in the days to come. Alas, the wise man will die just like the fool!

text.en: Verse 24 says the ability to eat, drink, and enjoy work is 'from the hand of God.' What does that phrase tell you about where such enjoyment comes from?

It tells us the enjoyment is a gift from God, not something a person can produce alone: 'this is from the hand of God' (v.24), and v.25 adds, 'For apart from Him, who can eat and who can find enjoyment?'
As the passage is read, listen for the short verdict the Preacher repeats after several of his thoughts.
The recurring verdict is 'This too is futile' (vv.19, 21, 23), often lengthened to 'futile and a pursuit of the wind' (v.26). The refrain carries his running assessment of toil under the sun.
Nothing is better for a man than to eat and drink and enjoy his work. I have also seen that this is from the hand of God. (Ecclesiastes 2:24, BSB)

And I saw that wisdom exceeds folly, just as light exceeds darkness: The wise man has eyes in his head, but the fool walks in darkness. Yet I also came to realize that one fate overcomes them both. (Ecclesiastes 2:13-14, BSB)
Finish the comparison from verse 13 from memory: 'And I saw that wisdom exceeds folly, just as ___.'
'...just as light exceeds darkness' (v.13).

verse.en: And I saw that wisdom exceeds folly, just as light exceeds darkness:

text.en: Retell the whole passage in your own words: how the Preacher weighed wisdom against folly, why he came to hate his toil, and where his thought finally landed.

A full retelling covers: the Preacher turning to consider wisdom and folly and granting that 'wisdom exceeds folly' (vv.12-13); the leveling realization that 'one fate overcomes them both' — both die and are forgotten (vv.14-16); his hatred of life and toil because he must leave all he built to a successor who may be wise or foolish (vv.17-21); the grief and sleepless nights such labor brings (vv.22-23); and the turn to the good that is 'from the hand of God' — to eat, drink, and enjoy one's work, given to the one pleasing in His sight (vv.24-26).
Verse 26 says the sinner gathers and accumulates 'that which he will hand over to one who pleases God.' How does Proverbs 13:22 state this same pattern?

Proverbs 13:22 (named in the brief) says, 'the sinner's wealth is passed to the righteous.' It states the same providential pattern as Ecclesiastes 2:26 — what the sinner heaps up is stored up for the one who pleases God. Scripture interprets Scripture.
Verse 24 says 'Nothing is better for a man than to eat and drink and enjoy his work.' How does Ecclesiastes 3:22 repeat this same conclusion later in the book?

Ecclesiastes 3:22 (named in the brief) says, 'there is nothing better for a man than to enjoy his work, because that is his lot.' It echoes the verdict of 2:24 — enjoying one's God-given work as one's portion. The refrain recurs across the book.

The Preacher finds that wisdom pursued 'under the sun' still ends in death and is forgotten (vv.14-16). How does 1 Corinthians 1:30-31 point beyond that horizon to where true wisdom and boasting are found?

1 Corinthians 1:30-31 (named in the brief) says Christ 'has become for us wisdom from God' and, 'Let him who boasts boast in the Lord.' Where the Preacher's wisdom under the sun cannot escape death, true wisdom is Christ, and the only lasting glory is in the Lord — beyond the horizon of vv.14-16.
When you hear the Preacher say his hard work felt 'futile,' what is one thing you would like to ask him about it?

Receive the child's own question; do not supply an answer. Good wonderings might touch why the work felt empty, what he was hoping to gain, or whether anything he did still mattered. Write the question down to carry into the reading.

The passage moves from despair over toil (vv.20-23) to 'Nothing is better... than to eat and drink and enjoy his work' (v.24). What question does that sudden turn raise for you that you would want to explore?

Invite the learner's own question; resist answering it for them. The aim is to surface genuine wondering about how the same labor can be both vanity and, received from God's hand, a gift to enjoy — not to test recall.

Hold verse 13, 'wisdom exceeds folly, just as light exceeds darkness,' against verse 14, 'one fate overcomes them both.' What real advantage does the Preacher grant to wisdom, and what limit does he then place on it?

First anchor to the text: he concedes a genuine advantage — wisdom truly 'exceeds folly, just as light exceeds darkness' (v.13); the wise man 'has eyes in his head' while 'the fool walks in darkness' (v.14). Then he limits it: 'one fate overcomes them both' (v.14) — death levels them, and 'the wise man will die just like the fool' (v.16). Wisdom is better to have, yet it cannot buy exemption from death. Keep the point to the text's own concession-then-limit; do not resolve it beyond what the passage states.

After all the talk of vanity, verse 24 says of eating, drinking, and enjoying work, 'this is from the hand of God.' How does naming God shift the passage from despair to something the Preacher can call good?

Anchor first to what the text says: the enjoyment of ordinary work and food is 'from the hand of God' (v.24), and 'apart from Him' none can find it (v.25). Naming God is the hinge: the same toil that was 'futile' under the sun becomes a gift when received from His hand. The turn is not to a new labor but to a new source — God's providence and grace. Meaning drawn from the text's own hinge, not speculation.
Verses 18-21 tell why the Preacher hated his toil. According to the text, what was it about leaving his work behind that troubled him so deeply?

Anchor to the text: he hated his toil because 'I must leave it to the man who comes after me' (v.18), and 'who knows whether that man will be wise or foolish?' (v.19). One who 'has not worked for it' receives the portion he built 'with wisdom, knowledge, and skill' (v.21) — 'this too is futile and a great evil.' What troubles him is the loss of control over the fruit of his skilled labor once he is gone. Keep the reading to what the passage states.

On an index card, write one part of your daily work or study that you will deliberately receive this week as a gift 'from the hand of God' (v.24), and beside it write one specific way you will give thanks for it. Keep the card where you will see it.

Doable on paper with an index card and pencil. Ties v.24's 'from the hand of God' into a concrete, thankful use of one's calling. Note the planned action and the thanksgiving as observable evidence; never record a judgment about the learner's gratitude or spiritual state.

Verse 23 describes labor so anxious that 'even at night, his mind does not rest.' Name one specific task you tend to carry into the night, and one concrete thing you will do this week to set it down and rest.

Look for a concrete, doable step tied to a real task named by the learner (v.23). Record only the observable plan; do not assess the learner's anxiety, faith, or character. The aim is the ordered, cheerful use of one's calling, not a verdict on the heart.

Draw a picture of one good thing you enjoy that is a gift from God — food you like to eat, or a job you help with at home. Point to your picture and tell what you drew and how you can say thank you to God for it.

Ties verse 24's good gifts 'from the hand of God' into a doable drawing with paper and crayons. Affirm the concrete thing the child names and their idea of giving thanks; record what they drew as observable evidence, never a judgment about the child.
In this passage, who appoints a time for every purpose, lays the burden upon men, and makes everything beautiful in its time?
God. He appoints "a time for every purpose under heaven" (3:1), "has laid upon the sons of men" their burden (3:10), and "has made everything beautiful in its time" (3:11).
The Preacher says a burden has been laid on people to occupy them, and that God has set eternity in their hearts. On whom does the passage say these things fall?

On "the sons of men" / "men": God "has laid upon the sons of men" the burden "to occupy them" (3:10), and "has also set eternity in the hearts of men" (3:11). The one who toils is also called "the worker" (3:9).
After the long list of paired 'times' (3:2-8), what question does the Preacher ask next, and what does he turn to consider after asking it?

He first asks, "What does the worker gain from his toil?" (3:9). He then turns from the catalogue of times to reflect on God's work: the burden God has laid on men (3:10), that God "has made everything beautiful in its time" and set eternity in the heart (3:11), the good of rejoicing and finding satisfaction as God's gift (3:12-13), and that God's work endures forever so that men should fear Him (3:14-15).
The passage repeats the words 'season' and 'time' (3:1). In the sense this passage gives them, does a 'time' mean a moment we happen to choose, or something else? Explain from the text.

A 'time' or 'season' here means a divinely fixed, appointed span — not human choice or chance. The passage grounds every 'time' in God: there is "a time for every purpose under heaven" (3:1), and God "has made everything beautiful in its time" (3:11). The paired opposites (3:2-8) are seasons that each take their turn under God's appointment, which is why toil adds no lasting gain (3:9) and God's work "endures forever" (3:14).
Verse 11 says God "has made everything beautiful in its time." What do the words "in its time" add to the meaning here?

"In its time" means each thing is fitting and lovely in its own proper season — as cold suits winter and heat suits summer. God's beauty is not that every event is pleasant, but that each is set in the right season by His ordering, so it becomes "beautiful in its time." This matches the appointed 'seasons' of 3:1-8.
Listen for the two small words that begin almost every line in the first part of the reading. What word keeps coming back before 'to be born,' 'to plant,' 'to laugh'?

"A time" — the phrase "a time to..." begins each half of the paired opposites in 3:2-8 (e.g., "a time to be born and a time to die," "a time to weep and a time to laugh"). "Time" (with "season") is the passage's key repeated term (3:1).
To everything there is a season, and a time for every purpose under heaven: (Ecclesiastes 3:1)
He has made everything beautiful in its time. He has also set eternity in the hearts of men, yet they cannot fathom the work that God has done from beginning to end. (Ecclesiastes 3:11)
Finish the line from verse 11: 'He has made everything ____ in its time.'
"beautiful" — "He has made everything beautiful in its time" (3:11).

This passage ends by saying God "will call to account what has passed" (3:15). Two verses later, Ecclesiastes 3:17 picks up the same thought. What does Ecclesiastes 3:17 say God will do, and how does it use the idea of appointed 'time'?

Ecclesiastes 3:17 says, in the Preacher's heart, that "God will judge the righteous and the wicked, since there is a time for every activity and every deed." It applies the same appointed-'time' theme of 3:1-8 to judgment: just as every purpose has its season, so God's judging has its set time. This confirms 3:15 that God "will call to account what has passed."

Verse 11 says God "has made everything beautiful in its time," showing His ordered wisdom. Romans 1:19-20 points to another place people can see God's wisdom and power. Where does Romans say it is seen?

Romans 1:19-20 says that God's invisible qualities — His eternal power and divine nature — have been clearly seen since the creation of the world, being understood from what He has made, so that men are without excuse. As Ecclesiastes sees God's ordered wisdom in making everything "beautiful in its time" (3:11), Romans sees it in the created world — Scripture interpreting Scripture.

The Preacher says God "has also set eternity in the hearts of men, yet they cannot fathom the work that God has done from beginning to end" (3:11). What questions does this raise for you about the things you long to understand but cannot?

Open item — invite the learner's own wondering; do not supply a settled answer. Good directions to welcome: why God gives us a sense of eternity yet not full understanding; what it means to trust God with what we cannot fathom (3:11); how this drives us to "fear Him" (3:14) rather than to despair. Keep the response the learner's own; record what they ask, not a verdict on their faith.
The poem names many opposite times — "a time to weep and a time to laugh, a time to mourn and a time to dance." What is one thing you would want to ask about how God gives us these different times?

Open item — the child's own question is the goal; do not answer it for them. It touches the appointed 'seasons' of 3:1-8 and God making everything "beautiful in its time" (3:11). Welcome wonder about weeping and laughing seasons alike; write down the question the child asks. Evidence, not judgment.

The passage lists opposite pairs such as "a time to kill and a time to heal" and "a time for war and a time for peace." Read alongside verse 1 and verse 11, what one truth does the whole list teach — and why would it be a mistake to read it as approving both sides of every pair equally?

Anchor first in the text: "To everything there is a season, and a time for every purpose under heaven" (3:1) and "He has made everything beautiful in its time" (3:11). The one truth: every event and condition of life has a season fixed by God's sovereign appointment, and He orders each in its proper time. The pairs illustrate that single truth — the ceaseless turns of a life we cannot control. They are not a moral endorsement of both terms (the passage is not commending killing or hating); it observes that each has its appointed season under God, driving us to "fear Him" (3:14). Reading move: read each pair as an instance of the one sovereign appointment, not as ethics. Keep to what the text states (WCF 1.9).
Verse 14 says "everything God does endures forever; nothing can be added to it or taken from it." According to the same verse, why does God do it this way — what does the text say His purpose is?

The text states it plainly: "God does it so that they should fear Him" (3:14). Anchor: God's work "endures forever" and cannot be added to or diminished (3:14); its unchangeable permanence is meant to lead men to fear (reverence and awe) Him. This is the end God intends by the mystery of His providence — meaning drawn from the verse itself, not speculation.
Retell this passage in your own words: the many appointed 'times' of life, the question about what the worker gains, and what the Preacher then says about God's work and the good gift God gives.

A faithful retelling should include: every purpose has its God-appointed season (3:1), shown by the paired opposites (3:2-8); the worker gains no lasting profit from his toil (3:9), a burden God has laid on men (3:10); yet God makes everything beautiful in its time and has set eternity in the heart, though man cannot fathom His whole work (3:11); so the good is to rejoice, do good, eat, drink, and find satisfaction in labor as God's gift (3:12-13); and God's work endures forever, unchangeable, so that men should fear Him (3:14-15). Do not require exact wording; listen for these movements. Record what the learner recalls, not a judgment of them.

Verses 12-13 say there is nothing better than "to rejoice and do good" and to "find satisfaction" in our work as God's gift. What is one specific good thing you could do this week, and one task you could do thankfully instead of grumbling?

Application — look for a concrete, doable action, not a feeling or self-assessment. Anchor: "there is nothing better for them than to rejoice and do good while they live" and to "eat and drink and find satisfaction in all his labor—this is the gift of God" (3:12-13). Good answers name a specific deed (help a sibling, thank a parent) and a specific task done with gladness. Record the observable plan; do not grade the child's heart.

On an index card, write one 'time' from the poem that fits something happening in your family right now (for example, "a time to mend" or "a time to plant"). On the back, write one good, doable thing you can do in that season this week.

Paper activity — an index card and pencil are enough. It moves the appointed 'times' of 3:1-8 into the learner's present life and pairs it with the 3:12-13 good of doing good and working faithfully as God's gift. Look for a concrete action tied to a real family season. Keep the record to what was written and planned; observe action, never assess character.
Draw a picture of one 'time' from the reading — a time to plant, a time to laugh, or a time to dance. Then act it out for your family.

Paper-and-crayon activity for pre-readers; the drawing and acting-out are the observable response. It fixes one of the appointed 'times' (3:2-4) in memory and joins it to the passage's call to "rejoice and do good" (3:12). No reading required — an adult can name the times aloud. Record which 'time' the child chose and drew; do not assess the child.
